## [03:01 - 06:01]

All the people are going to stay the same in there. However, there's tools that you can use where it will be dynamically updated. And in fact, when you integrate with MailChimp, that's what happens. And every day you have about maybe 10 or 15 new people that subscribe to your email list. So if you create an ad and a campaign and you're targeting people on your email list, well, what's going to happen is at first it's going to go really, really well because no one on that list has seen that ad before, right? And then what you're probably going to find is after even maybe a week, two weeks, three weeks, your results are going to start to dwindle. And that's because your audience, while yes, you're getting about 10 to 15 new people on that list every day, overall it's pretty static that there's a big base audience there that has already seen your ad. And seeing the ad multiple times isn't necessarily going to improve results for you, right? So your ad may be working with the new people coming on your list, so the 10, 15 new people. And the rest of the people that have already seen your ads, it really doesn't do much good to keep showing them that ad, right? So that's one example of why we have what we call retargeting stacks so that it's evergreen. So you see how evergreen and retargeting stacks are almost one and the same. ## [30:31 - 33:33]

We're excluding those that visited in the last 20 days, which means we're actually only targeting people between days 11 and 20, which means now we've got, like now we're getting into an evergreen system, right? Where no one is staying static in one area. No one is going to be shown the same ad forever. There's exclusions at every level and they're essentially being funneled and shown different creative. Now you can go on and on and on and on with this, right? You can go on, like, visited blog days 30, visited blog days 40 and continue to exclude the last custom audience that preceded that. And every single one of those retargeting stacks is what we call them, is showing them a different message and ultimately with the purpose of trying to stay top of mind and continue to drive them to the website. Now what I would say here though is that if someone hasn't gone to the purchase page, depending on what industry you're in, so real estate, for example, has much longer lead-in times, so you do have to have longer retargeting stacks because it's an industry known for 90-day lead-in times, which is quite long. So high ticket e-commerce can have very long lead-in times as well. If you're selling something like pet food or puppy training type stuff, if someone hasn't visited the sales page after, say, like 40 days, I think you can kind of spit them out of your funnel. ## [36:37 - 39:42]

So anyone that purchased in the last, you know, after 30 days, let's say you're selling pet food. Well, pet food, we all know dog owners need to continue feeding their dogs, right? It's not like you buy one can of food once in your life and your dog is set for life. It's like a very renewable product like razors, like clothing can be like that as well. So unlike real estate, right, where someone might not buy another house or sell another house for many, many years, depending on your client, you may want to reactivate existing customers that purchased after 30 days. So you'd be showing them a totally different message. And those guys are usually really easy to convert. So don't miss the step if you're working with clients that are that type of industry where it's renewable, like people will constantly buy more and more of what they have. Because it's much easier and it's much more cost effective to sell to existing customers than it is to sell to a new audience. Because you can see here it's very intense to build customers, right? So that is a step that most businesses overlook. And you can't just rely on their email sequences. So a lot of them have email sequences where they try to reactivate customers. Email is still one of the highest converting things out there. It works, but the open rates are pretty shitty. So just imagine what that would do to complement that with the retargeting ad, right? Without confusing you guys, another audience you shouldn't overlook is people that message their page. So people that sent them a message on Messenger, okay? So you can take this funnel much further. And I think what I'm just trying to say is don't overlook retargeting existing customers. ## [39:42 - 42:45]

Now if you have a client that is spending like 20, 40, 50, 100, 200K a month in ad spend, you will have a lot of people in this funnel. So to give you an idea, with the FATC funnel that I'm running at the moment, top of funnel at any one time, or sorry, middle of funnel, so people that are opting in, so we lead the funnel with a freebie, an opt-in. At any one time, there's probably an audience in the middle of funnel, so people that opted in of about 5,000 people, 4,000 people. So it's very, very big, well it's a big, warm audience anyways. So I'm able to get a bit more granular here, where I can actually break that down like days one to five, days six to seven. If you're, and actually that's not even that big, because some clients will have massive retargeting audiences, and if that's the case, you can get as specific as like days one to three, days four to five, days da da da. Now what I would say however, is as you start to get towards the bottom of the funnel, what we are seeing across your own campaigns, and across the campaigns that we work with with clients, is that a very high frequency of the same ad tends to work really, really well, especially in e-commerce and online courses. So a high frequency, meaning you're actually hammering them very, very often with the same message, tends to work really, really well. However, you still want to make sure that you're not doing that for like 180 days, because if someone hasn't bought from you after 30 days of high frequency, they need to see a different message. They're not buying from you, you know, you need to show them something else. But what I'm getting at here is that you probably want to aim for a high frequency at the bottom of the funnel, and you have probably a better tolerance of a longer retargeting stack. So here you could make it a lot shorter, and your aim is like, I just want to get you to the sales page. So I'm just going to show you as many fucking different messages, and one of them is going to drive you to that sales page. exclude our 15, sorry, we'll exclude purchases 30 days, and we'll also exclude visited sales page 15 days. This might be like free shipping or something, I don't know, or a testimonial, or reviews. So this is essentially an evergreen funnel, because someone comes in, a cold person comes in, and they're basically being funneled through the entire sequence. And if they don't perform a certain action within a certain specific time, they actually get spat out, and that's probably the wrong word because it sounds so rude, but they get funneled out of your funnel. When you start to get in here, it doesn't tend to matter so much because they're pretty warm at this stage. But one of the tricks that you can do, which I talk about quite a bit guys and I highly recommend that you guys do that, is you actually social proof. A really, really great hack here is to actually target existing customers. So people that target that purchase. So you target that ad to existing customers because typically what will happen is those existing customers are going to comment on those ads or go like, my God, I love my leggings or my God, my dog loves this food. My God, this is the best thing ever. And what that does for anyone that's kind of fresh in your funnel is it's like the ultimate stamp of approval for them because it's like instead of

## [54:54 - 57:55]

a marketer telling me this is the best dog food, it's like all of a sudden, people that they associate with their peers are saying this is the best dog food ever. So it's a very, very powerful trick to target social proof those ads by targeting it to customers. You can even target it to page fans. So page fans are fairly warm audiences. So, I mean, there's quite a bit to cover here, but this is very similar to retargeting stacks. So when we ran the training two weeks ago, we were really just looking at this. And an evergreen funnel, all that really is, is when you look at it and there's lots of different funnel structures, every industry, every niche is going to have different funnel structures or what's going to work top of funnel and etc. But the main point is that you're constantly putting lots of people in at the top and instead of stagnating like one message to the same group of people over a prolonged period of time, you're shifting them through a series of different messages across your funnel and freeing them or spitting them out if they don't perform a certain action after a specific amount of time. So that you're only focusing your budget on where the good, where the ROI really is. In terms of budget, typically what we would do is 75% of our budget would go towards top of funnel. So that's normally how we would break it down. And then you'd find around your bottom of funnel that's, it would be very, very small proportion of your budget as you get down to those levels. What you should always be doing with your clients is at least every two to three days or if you're very busy at least once a week is when you're running funnels like this, spend some time to go into the ad account, bring up yesterday's performance results at your bottom of funnel and middle of funnel. So go through and look at your middle of funnel and bottom of funnel, pull up yesterday's spend and see what is your daily budget, how much has Facebook spent. So let's say your daily budget is 15 bucks and Facebook spent $15.35. It means it's hungry for more. You need to start scaling that. So start scaling your bottom of funnel. Because if you don't scale your bottom of funnel, you're actually just going to spend a lot of money and not get your client results, right? Because this is where sales come in the cheapest. So you need to make sure that you are constantly looking to see, especially if you're scaling at the top here, you need to make sure you're also scaling at the bottom. You just simply look at is it hungry for more budget? Is Facebook spending all the budget I'm giving it? Can it handle more budget? If so, scale it. Is it better to do daily budget or lifetime budget?

## [57:55 - 59:50]

So I mean, Facebook technically prefers lifetime budget because it prefers evergreen stuff. So it wants to know that you're not going to be showing the same people something forever. And by definition, lifetime budgets mean that you're not going to do that, right? Because you either need to retop it or change or you're doing something else. So it's a finite period of time. So for that reason, Facebook has slight preferences over lifetime budgets. It's nothing really that big. So you're not going to notice a massive difference in the results of your campaigns. Lifetime budgets are harder to scale. So that's one thing to be aware of. And they need a bit more hands on and they feed out very fun, like very strangely. So sometimes you might set a lifetime budget of $200. And your expectation would be, well, Facebook is going to spend roughly $10 a day on this. And you might find like the first week it only spends like 30 bucks, right? And then it's going to go into like accelerated spend in the last week to try and get rid of that budget. So it does have some funny quirks around it. I would recommend if you're a beginner, if you're getting started out, if you're a freelancer, if you're a lone wolf, stick to daily budgets because they're much easier to manage. And the differences are probably not large enough until you start working with like large ad spends with clients with massive, massive ad spends. If you've got a team, if you're an agency owner and you've got a team, then definitely if you can be a bit more hands on about it, lifetimes, they're a bit more sensitive. So, but yes, if you're resting your audiences, like what we talked about at the beginning of this call, then you would use lifetime budget for that. Okay, so without further ado, I'm going to stop sharing my...
